title: Competency Portfolio and First-6mo Tooling Roadmap
preview:
Overview:

This document defines the competency portfolio approach used at Redwood for the first six months of employment, and the practical tooling and access roadmap that ensures new hires can execute against both onboarding deliverables and early career growth goals. It is intended for new hires, hiring managers, people-ops partners, and engineering managers who own role activation and early-career development.

Purpose:
- Reduce time-to-impact by making role responsibilities, success criteria, and required tools explicit for months 0–6.
- Tie access and task milestones to measurable competency checkpoints that map to the company role ladder.
- Provide reusable templates for managers to run consistent, equitable onboarding and early development cycles.

Scope and audience:
- Applies to full-time engineering, product, and core platform hires. Individual contributor (IC) and manager flows are described; managers should adapt for discipline-specific needs (e.g., Embedding infra vs. SDK docs).
- Does not replace role-specific technical onboarding created by teams; it complements those docs with standard tooling and competency expectations.

High-level timeline (first 6 months):
1) Day 0 (pre-start / first hours): account activation and baseline reading.
2) Week 1: contributor setup, small PR + buddy pairing, first retrospective.
3) Month 1: deliver first scoped task, competency checkpoint #1 (operational readiness).
4) Month 3: broader cross-team project contribution, competency checkpoint #2 (domain proficiency).
5) Month 6: graduation review, career conversation aligned to role ladder and next-sprint goals.

Role setup checklist (Day 0 -> Week 1):
- Day -7 to 0: HR completes offer checklist and submits access requests to IAM. Hiring manager schedules 1:1 cadence and assigns a buddy.
- Day 0: laptop and credentials delivered; verify MFA, SSO; join company Slack, add to team channel.
- Day 1: walkthrough of team README, critical repos, and run the demo app locally (if applicable).
- End of Week 1: submit first PR (documentation or minor bug), attend onboarding retro.

Tools and access matrix (practical table for owners and timelines):
(Columns: Tool | Typical roles | Access by | Initial owner | Notes)
- GitHub | Engineering, Docs | Day 0–1 | infra-identity | Required repo access gated by team membership; follow code ownership docs.
- Jira / Ticketing | Eng, Product, SRE | Day 0–3 | hiring-manager | Default boards added; request custom board access once ownership confirmed.
- Slack | Everyone | Day 0 | people-ops | Add to security and team channels; create a dedicated onboarding channel for the hire.
- Redwood Console (internal) | Eng, Product | Day 1–7 | platform-ops | Grants: read-only initially, escalate to write for owners after Month 1 checkpoint.
- KMS / Secrets | Eng (prn) | Post probation (policy) | security | Access requires manager sign-off and audit log annotation.
- Buildkite / CI | Eng | Week 1 | infra-ci | Submit a test CI job as part of Week 1 checklist.
- Google Drive / Notion | Everyone | Day 0 | people-ops | Central onboarding folder contains checklists and role templates.

Competency portfolio and checkpoint definitions (explicit, measurable):
- Competency A: Operational Readiness (Month 1 checkpoint)
  - Indicators: can run local dev environment, open and merge a documentation PR, demonstrate safe deploy/run procedures in a sandbox.
  - Evidence: PR link, sandbox run command output snippets, checklist signed by buddy.
- Competency B: Domain Proficiency (Month 3 checkpoint)
  - Indicators: closed a customer-impacting ticket or owned a small feature end-to-end, demonstrated design knowledge in a tech review.
  - Evidence: Issue/PR links, short design note, manager sign-off.
- Competency C: Cross-team Collaboration & Reliability (Month 6 graduation)
  - Indicators: led a cross-functional sync, contributed to team SLA or postmortem, proactive quality improvements proposed.
  - Evidence: meeting notes, postmortem doc link, proposed improvement tracked in Jira.

Mapping competencies to role ladder (sample guidance):
- Associate IC (L1–L2): Month 6 should show clear Operational Readiness and early Domain Proficiency indicators. Career talk focuses on consistency and scope growth.
- Experienced IC (L3): Expected to reach Domain Proficiency by Month 3 and show Cross-team Collaboration evidence by Month 6.
- Senior IC (L4+): Early evidence of cross-team impact and coaching; manager may validate accelerated milestones for lateral hires.

Manager playbook (practical steps and cadence):
1) Preboarding: submit access requests, schedule 1:1 for first 4 weeks, pick buddy.
2) Week 0/1: complete the role setup checklist with the hire; ensure PR and CI run completed.
3) End of Month 1: run competency review (15–30m), document evidence and set up remediation work if needed (concrete tasks).
4) Month 3 & 6: formal checkpoint conversations using the Competency Portfolio template; update performance notes in the HR system and propose next-sprint goals.

Templates and artifacts (copy-and-paste ready):
- 30/60/90 day plan outline (title: "Impact Roadmap")
- Competency Evidence Log (bullet list with links and manager sign-off line)
- Manager conversation prompts (structured questions to calibrate expectations)

Onboarding schedule examples (two-week snapshot):
Day 0–1: paperwork, accounts, team intro, runbook review.
Day 2–5: local build, first PR, embedded pair with engineer on bug triage.
Week 2: shadow sprint planning, own a small ticket, join security intro session.

Equity and accessibility considerations:
- All tooling requests should have alternatives for assistive tech. People-ops will prioritize hardware accommodations within two business days.
- If a candidate needs extended ramp time, managers must flag people-ops and create an individualized plan; competency timelines are guidelines, not gatekeepers.

Ownership and escalation:
- People-ops: owning the master onboarding checklist and central documents.
- Hiring manager: owning the role-specific onboarding and competency sign-offs.
- Security & Infra: approving access to sensitive systems and KMS/secrets flows.
- Escalation: if a hire is not meeting Month 1 checkpoint, manager escalates to the People-ops partner with documented remediation plan within 5 business days.

Measuring effectiveness (KPIs for this program):
- Median time-to-first-merged-PR (goal: <= 10 days for engineering hires).
- % of hires meeting Month 1 checkpoint (goal: 85%).
- Manager satisfaction (quarterly survey) and new-hire NPS at Day 30 and Day 90.

FAQ (brief):
Q: What if a hired role requires special hardware?
A: Manager requests hardware via the People-ops procurement flow; expedite flag for critical lab machines is available.

Q: Can competency checkpoints be moved for lateral hires?
A: Yes. Managers document agreed-upon timelines in the Competency Evidence Log and notify People-ops.

Links and references:
- Onboarding toolkit and checklists: /confluence/company-handbook/05_internal-tools/onboarding-toolchain-access-and-career-checkpoints-2025
- Path to impact (120-day guide): /confluence/company-handbook/04_hiring-and-onboarding/path-to-impact-new-hire-120-day-ops-and-benefit-orientation-2023
- Role ladder docs: /confluence/company-handbook/02_roles-and-career/competency-maps-and-rotation-paths-2027

Revision history:
- 2028-11-14: Initial draft authored by Amaya Chen.
- 2029-01-22: Incorporated manager feedback and explicit KMS gating steps (review by Security).
- 2029-02-12: Final edits and published; added KPIs and FAQ entries.

Acknowledgements:
Thanks to the People-ops onboarding team, Platform Engineering, and Security for contributions and review.

Appendix (quick CLI snippets and sample checklist):
- Sample local build command: `./scripts/dev-start --env sandbox`
- Minimal checklist item (copy to PR description): "Runs locally, unit tests pass, link to issue, reviewed by buddy, CI green."

title: New-Joiner Systems & Career Activation Manual
preview:
Overview
--------
This manual consolidates the practical systems, access steps, and role-aligned activation checkpoints every new Redwood engineer or product hire needs to get productive quickly. It complements the People Ops orientation and the team-specific 30/90 plans by focusing on the *systems* and *career activation points* — accounts, permissions, tooling, and the milestones that unlock career progress.

Use this as a single-source checklist during the first 6 months; owners and tooling contacts are listed for each item.

Scope
-----
- Audience: all new full-time and contractor hires across Engineering, Product, and Platform teams.
- Exclusions: individual contributor interview/hiring guides, team-specific onboarding curricula (those live on team wikis).
- Outcomes: verified access to core systems, completed security & compliance steps, and alignment on career ladder milestones.

Quick start checklist (first 7 days)
----------------------------------
1. Confirm HR accounts & payroll: complete I-9 and direct deposit in HR portal.
2. Badge and workspace: collect building access badge and desk assignment.
3. Slack, SSO, and MFA: verify Slack workspace, Okta SSO, and enroll hardware or software MFA.
4. GitHub & monorepo: confirm GitHub membership for your team org and access to relevant repos.
5. Cloud console access: request least-privilege AWS/GCP project membership for dev environment.
6. Redwood Console demo: sign in to the internal Redwood Console sandbox and run the example inference call.
7. Manager 1:1: schedule weekly 1:1 for first quarter to review goals and blockers.

Access matrix (who to request from)
----------------------------------
| System | Typical owner | Request channel | SLA | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---:|---|
| Okta / SSO | People Ops (identity) | identity-requests@redwood.internal (ticket) | 24h | MFA enforced; new employees receive activation link automatically |
| Slack / Email | IT Support | it-support ticket | 4h | Join #all-company, #engineering, and team channel by default |
| GitHub (org) | Eng Platform | platform-access@redwood.internal (ticket) | 48h | Include repo names and expected role (read/write/admin) |
| AWS/GCP dev project | Cloud Infra | infra-requests Slack + ticket | 48h | Provide justification; least-privilege role applied |
| Redwood Console (sandbox) | Developer Experience | dex@redwood.internal | 24h | Sandbox includes sample API key and quickstart prompts |
| Payroll/Benefits portal | People Ops | hr-portal | 72h | Enrollment deadlines outlined in Benefits section |

First 30/60/90/180 roadmap (activation + career signals)
------------------------------------------------------
- First 30 days (activation):
  1) Complete all access steps in Quick start checklist.
  2) Merge your first non-doc change (small bugfix/README edit) and submit a retrospective with your mentor.
  3) Complete security & compliance trainings: InfoSec orientation, data handling, and code of conduct.

- First 60 days (contribution):
  1) Ship a scoped enhancement or bugfix owned end-to-end in your team’s backlog.
  2) Present a short demo at the team retro or demo hour.
  3) Request feedback from your manager; define 90-day goals.

- First 90 days (impact):
  1) Lead a cross-functional story or own a small service component.
  2) Schedule a calibration checkpoint with manager and mentor to review promotion-readiness signals (see Role Ladder Signals).

- First 180 days (career activation):
  1) Submit a self-assessment tied to the Role Competency Map.
  2) If aiming for accelerated growth, discuss stretch assignments with manager and People Ops.

Role ladder signals (how systems map to career progress)
-------------------------------------------------------
- Individual Contributor (IC) levels: early ICs should demonstrate consistent delivery, ownership of an API/feature, plus clear documentation and mentoring of peers.
- Senior ICs: wider cross-team influence, shipping platform-level changes, and drive reliability/perf improvements measured by dashboards.
- Staff/Principal: create multi-team strategy, own long-lived architecture changes, or lead major product initiatives.

Career signals checklist (examples to document in self-assessment):
- Owned features with measurable business metrics (latency, cost, adoption).
- Mentorship: number of mentees and documented outcomes.
- Reliability: introduced runbooks or reduced incident MTTR by defined percentage.
- Cost/efficiency: implemented batching/caching changes that decreased per-token cost.

Benefits & perks quick links and deadlines
---------------------------------------
- Health insurance enrollment: complete within 30 days of start date. Link: hr-portal > Benefits.
- Flexible work & stipends: apply via perks-ops form during first 60 days.
- Training budget: managers approve training reimbursements up to $2,000/year; submit through learning@redwood.internal.

Internal tools: short how-tos and owner contacts
---------------------------------------------
- GitHub monorepo: clone with SSH. Example:
  $ git clone git@github.com:redwood-inference/core.git
  - Owners: Eng Platform (platform-access@redwood.internal)

- Local dev (inference sandbox):
  1. Install rdx-cli: pip install rdx-cli==0.9.4
  2. Run: rdx login --sso
  3. Start local server: make dev-run
  - Known constraints: CUDA 12 required for GPU integration tests. See dev/README.md for hardware matrix.

- Requesting production credentials (least-privilege flow):
  1. Open infra-requests ticket with service name and justification.
  2. Submit a short runbook and rollback plan.
  3. Security owner will approve or request changes within SLA.

Sample access request template (copy/paste)
-----------------------------------------
Subject: Access request — [Your Name] — [System]
Body:
- Name: [Your Name]
- Team: [Team Name]
- Needs access to: [System/Repo/Project]
- Justification: [Short reason]
- Expected duration: [Permanent / 3 months]
- Manager: [Manager Name]

Manager checklist for activation
--------------------------------
1. Confirm new hire has completed HR onboarding items and badge pickup.
2. Ensure Jira/Backlog access and assign first sprint tickets.
3. Introduce to mentor and schedule weekly syncs.
4. Review and approve training requests in first 90 days.

Escalations & service owners
---------------------------
- Identity/SSO issues: Maya Chen (People Ops) — identity-requests@redwood.internal — 24h SLA
- Cloud infra access: Arjun Patel (Cloud Infra) — infra-requests@redwood.internal — 48h SLA
- Developer tooling / GitHub: Eng Platform — platform-access@redwood.internal — 48h SLA
- Security concerns: Sofia Martinez (InfoSec) — security@redwood.internal — immediate alert via PagerDuty

Updating this manual
--------------------
- Owner: People Ops (Maya Chen). Annual review every January and ad-hoc updates for major tooling changes.
- Change process: submit a PR to this page with the proposed change, tag reviewers, and notify #people-ops when ready to publish.

Appendix A — Recommended early prompts to try in Redwood Console sandbox
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
1. "Generate a short summary of this PR and list 3 action items."
2. "Create a test prompt set to validate hallucination rate on the FAQ model."

Appendix B — Example career self-assessment template
---------------------------------------------------
- Summary of contributions (bulleted)
- Metrics / outcomes (links to dashboards)
- Mentorship & cross-team work
- Goals for next 6 months
- Areas of support requested (training, stretch assignment)

Contact & feedback
------------------
For corrections to this manual or to propose improvements, email identity-requests@redwood.internal with subject line: 'Manual feedback — New-Joiner Systems'.

This document is intended to be practical and prescriptive; teams should adapt team-specific onboarding while ensuring these system activation steps are complete.

title: Growth Micro-Paths and Skill Sprint Handbook
preview:
Overview
This handbook defines Redwood's Growth Micro-Paths and Skill Sprints program: a lightweight, time-boxed set of learning and delivery activities (1–6 weeks) that let contributors (ICs and managers) gain targeted skills, demonstrate growth toward a ladder milestone, and produce a small, shippable artifact that benefits a team or product.

Why we run micro-paths
- Fast, measurable skill development aligned to ladder competencies.
- Low-friction way to pilot cross-team rotations and internal mobility.
- Provides input signals to performance calibration (work samples) without long-term role changes.

Scope and audience
- Audience: Individual contributors, new managers, hiring managers, people-ops partners, and engineering leads.
- Scope: Technical and non-technical micro-paths (e.g., inference performance tuning, product analytics, onboarding docs, customer playbooks, security reviews).

Program goals (success metrics)
- Participation: 30% of ICs engage in at least one micro-path per year.
- Outcome quality: 80% of sprints produce an artifact accepted by an owner team.
- Mobility signal: 25% of participants use sprint artifacts in level reviews.
- Time-to-value: average sprint duration <= 3 weeks.

Micro-Path catalog (examples)
(Use this catalog as templates; teams can propose new micro-paths using the intake form.)
- \"Inference Latency Triage (2 wks)\" — owner: Eng-serving-runtime. Output: triage doc + perf dashboard patch. Key skills: profiling, perf regression analysis, instrumentation.
- \"Product-Led Experiment Design (3 wks)\" — owner: Product. Output: experiment spec + monitoring runbook. Key skills: hypothesis framing, instrumentation, postmortem.
- \"Customer Onboarding Template (1 wks)\" — owner: Customer Success. Output: new standardized onboarding template and 1 pilot. Key skills: stakeholder interviews, docs authoring.
- \"Secure Deployment Checklist (4 wks)\" — owner: Security and Compliance. Output: checklist + one verified deployment. Key skills: secure design, threat modeling, audit evidence.

Sprint types and recommended lengths
- Deep Technical (3–6 weeks): complex infra or runtime work that requires code changes and benchmarks.
- Product/Experiments (2–4 weeks): measurable experiment or feature spec plus monitoring.
- Docs/Enablement (1–2 weeks): playbooks, templates, training content.
- Compliance/Controls (2–4 weeks): controls, checklists, artifacts for audits.

How to propose a micro-path (intake flow)
1) Fill the Growth Sprint Intake form (JIRA template: GROWTH-SPRINT). Include: objective, acceptance criteria, duration, proposed owner, and mentor.
2) People-ops assigns a mentor and flags the proposal to the proposed owner team for capacity confirmation (48 hours).
3) Owner team accepts or requests scope adjustments. If accepted, create a lightweight epic and schedule a kickoff.
4) Kickoff (30–60 min): align outcomes, list artifacts, confirm reviewer and evaluation criteria.
5) Execute sprint with weekly 30-min syncs.
6) Submit final artifact, request owner sign-off, and publish artifact to relevant knowledge base.

Roles and responsibilities
- Participant: drives the work, prepares artifacts, requests feedback, and documents outcomes.
- Mentor (assigned by people-ops): provides technical/product guidance and helps remove blockers.
- Owner team: reviews and accepts artifacts; integrates the artifact or documents reasons for rejection.
- People-ops: triage proposals, curate catalog, track participation metrics, and help surface artifacts in leveling cycles.

Sprint tooling checklist
- Create JIRA epic using the GROWTH-SPRINT template and tag: growth-sprint/YYYY-MM-DD.
- Add the sprint to the shared calendar: Team Growth Sprints (public).
- Use the sprint README template (see snippet below) as the primary artifact index.

Sprint README template (example)
\"\"\"
Title: <Sprint title>\
Owner: <Team>\
Participant(s): <Names>\
Mentor: <Name>\
Duration: <start — end>\
Acceptance criteria: 1) <deliverable> 2) <measurable success metric>\
Artifacts: link-to-prs, link-to-dashboards, link-to-docs
\"\"\"

CLI examples (artifact publishing)
- Create epic from template:
  jira create --template GROWTH-SPRINT --summary \"Inference Latency Triage\" --label growth-sprint
- Publish docs to knowledge base (internal CLI):
  kb publish ./sprint-readme.md --space company-handbook --topic \"growth-sprint\"

Evaluation and leveling integration
- Use artifacts as work samples during quarterly calibration and leveling conversations. Participants should submit: sprint README, code PRs, dashboards, and owner sign-off.
- Managers use the artifact checklist during 1:1s and promotion packets to demonstrate competency in relevant ladder skills.
- People-ops compiles a quarterly digest of notable artifacts to highlight cross-team contributions.

Compensation and benefits alignment
- Skill sprints count as recognized development time; managers should treat sprint time as work time for capacity planning.
- For contributors on parental leave or reduced schedules, people-ops supports adjusted sprint durations and asynchronous mentorship.
- Approved sprint contributions can be referenced in spot bonuses and recognition awards per the Recognition Guidelines.

Onboarding integration (new hires)
- Within the first 60 days, new technical hires are encouraged to complete a 1–2 week Docs/Enablement micro-path to: learn codebase, contribute to internal docs, and meet cross-functional partners.
- New managers complete a Product/Experiment micro-path to shadow planning and metrics design.

Quality gates and acceptance criteria (example)
Acceptance criteria should be explicit and measurable. Example for a monitoring sprint:
- Dashboard shows baseline vs. post-change metrics for the last 7 days.
- Alert added to team Slack channel with playbook link.
- Owner team approves PR and confirms deployment to staging.

Recordkeeping and artifact storage
- Canonical artifacts must live in: code PRs (GitHub), dashboards (Grafana/Observable links), and knowledge base (company-handbook/growth-sprints).
- People-ops maintains an index spreadsheet: growth-sprints-index (read-only) with columns: sprint_id, title, participant, owner_team, start_date, end_date, artifact_links, owner_signoff.

Privacy and confidentiality
- Default classification: internal. For any customer-sensitive or security-sensitive artifacts, the owner team must mark artifacts as restricted and follow Security and Compliance guidance for evidence handling.

Common pitfalls and mitigation
- Over-scoping: Limit outcomes to 1–3 measurable artifacts. Mitigation: mentor enforces scope in kickoff.
- Poor handoff: Document a clear integration story and required follow-up actions in the README.
- No reviewer: Owner teams must nominate a reviewer at intake. If none exist, people-ops will escalate.

Template: sprint intake checklist (to paste into JIRA description)
1) Objective (1–2 sentences)
2) Acceptance criteria (3 bullets)
3) Proposed duration and time commitment
4) Owner and mentor names
5) Deliverables and where they will live
6) Risks and dependencies

FAQ
Q: Are micro-paths mandatory?
A: No, they are optional career-development mechanisms supported by people-ops and managers.

Q: Can I run a sprint across multiple teams?
A: Yes. Choose an owner team for acceptance and nominate liaisons from other teams.

Q: How do micro-paths affect performance review timelines?
A: Artifacts can be included in any upcoming review cycle; they provide concrete evidence but do not replace regular performance conversations.

Related links
- Growth Sprint Intake form: (internal link) /jira/templates/GROWTH-SPRINT
- Sprint README template: (internal link) /company-handbook/growth-sprints/templates/readme.md
- Recognition Guidelines: (internal link) /company-handbook/05_internal-tools/recognition-guidelines-2026

Owner and review cadence
- Program owner: People Ops (Marina Ortiz).
- Quarterly review: People-ops + cross-functional advisory group (Eng, Product, Security) to review metrics, refine catalog, and propose new templates.

Appendix A — Example sprint timeline (3-week technical sprint)
Week 0: Intake approved, mentor assigned, kickoff scheduled.
Week 1: Profiling and quick wins; end-of-week checkpoint with owner.
Week 2: Implementation and dashboards; mid-week demo.
Week 3: Polish, documentation, and owner sign-off.

Appendix B — Artifact acceptance rubric (short)
- Completeness (0–3): Are acceptance criteria met?
- Reusability (0–2): Can the artifact be reused by others?
- Traceability (0–2): Are links to tests, dashboards, and PRs present?
- Owner confidence (0–3): Does the owner team accept artifact for integration?

If you have questions or want to propose a new micro-path, contact: people-ops@redwood.ai or open the Growth Sprint Intake JIRA.

title: Career Neighborhoods and Benefits Playbook — Neighborhoods, Ladders, and Access
preview:
Purpose

This playbook introduces Redwood's career neighborhoods model, the expectations that power our leveling and promotion decisions, a high-level benefits snapshot tied to role families, and the internal tools and access patterns managers and new hires need to move quickly. The goal is to reduce ambiguity: give managers and contributors a single reference for role scope, compensation components, promotion mechanics, and the operational steps for hiring/onboarding and role changes.

Scope and audience

- Audience: managers, ICs, people-ops partners, hiring managers.
- Scope: Career family organization, leveling rubric and example expectations, benefits summary, hiring & onboarding checklists, internal tools access matrix, promotion and rotation procedures.

Principles and design goals

1. Role neighborhoods make tradeoffs explicit: group adjacent specialties so lateral moves and rotations are predictable.
2. Leveling is impact-first: scope, influence, and autonomy matter more than years-in-role.
3. Total rewards transparency: components and typical target mix by seniority are documented.
4. Operational simplicity: common checklists and a single access flow reduce time-to-productivity.

Career neighborhoods (high level)

We organize roles into neighborhoods. Each neighborhood contains role families that share core success signals and leveling patterns. Neighborhood naming is intentionally human and manager-friendly.

- Platform & Infrastructure neighborhood: GPU infra, cloud infra, infra reliability, serving runtime engineers. Owner: Eng Infrastructure.
- Product & Design neighborhood: product management, UX, product design. Owner: Product.
- Applied ML & Research neighborhood: model ops, inference research, evals. Owner: Applied ML.
- Core Services & Ops neighborhood: SRE, platform services, security ops. Owner: Eng SRE/Security.
- Customer & Success neighborhood: solutions engineering, enterprise onboarding, account product specialists. Owner: Customer Success.
- Go-to-Market neighborhood: sales, marketing, partnerships. Owner: Go-to-Market.
- People & Finance neighborhood: people-ops, finance, legal, procurement. Owner: People Ops/Finance-Legal.

Why neighborhoods?

- Easier lateral mobility: neighborhoods share competency language and training so engineers can move with fewer unknowns.
- Better calibration: promotion committees review same signals within a neighborhood.
- Tighter benefits alignment: some benefits (e.g., professional development budgets, conferences) are tuned per neighborhood.

Leveling rubric — simplified at-a-glance table

This is a concise rubric used during manager write-ups and calibration packets. Detailed competency maps live in the related competency doc.

| Level | Typical tenure | Scope & impact | Autonomy & decision-making | Example deliverables | Feedback cadence |
|-------|----------------|----------------|---------------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| IC1 / Associate | 0–2y | Task/feature ownership within a single team | Executes to spec; requires close manager support | Shipping discrete features, bug fixes | Weekly 1:1s; 30/60/90 checkpoint |
| IC2 / Professional | 2–4y | Cross-feature ownership; influences team plans | Makes design choices with peer review | Leading medium-scope projects; mentoring interns | Bi-weekly 1:1s; quarterly calibration |
| IC3 / Senior | 4–7y | Multi-team scope; shapes roadmap in neighborhood | Autonomously leads initiatives; influences architecture | End-to-end projects, mentoring leads | Monthly leadership syncs; mid-year review |
| Staff / Principal | 7+ (or high impact) | Company-level programs; cross-org influence | Sets strategy; high autonomy; delegates execution | Platform components, cross-org platform playbooks | Quarterly exec reviews; promotion cycle input |


Note: People-ops and managers may exercise exceptions for early promotable ICs with disproportionate impact — rationale must be documented in the packet and reviewed during calibration.

Promotion process (step-by-step)

1. Manager initiates with candidate packet (updated role profile, rubric mapping, 3–6 concrete examples of impact).
2. Peer reviewers collect written feedback (minimum: 3 peers across teams).
3. People-ops validates comp band, eligibility, and any policy constraints (e.g., in-flight hiring freezes).
4. Promotion committee convenes (neighborhood-aligned reviewers + cross-neighborhood rep).
5. Decision recorded with written feedback and next-step development plan.

Timeline guidance: aim for a 4-week cycle from packet submission to decision for standard promotions. Extensions require owner approval.

Role change and temporary rotations

- Lateral move: manager-to-manager agreement, updated role profile, update to compensation only if comp band changes. People-ops must approve access/entitlement changes.
- Rotation (3–6 months): short written agreement specifying objectives, mentorship pairing, and success criteria. Rotation count limit: 1 concurrent rotation per individual.

Compensation and benefits — high-level snapshot

Total rewards philosophy: Redwood balances market-competitive base pay, variable components for performance/role, and benefits that reduce hidden costs of high-performance work (child care support, mental health, family leaves).

Compensation components (typical mix by seniority):
- Base salary: 60–85% of total cash (lower in very senior roles).
- Short-term variable (bonus or commission): 0–20% (higher for GTM roles).
- Equity / long-term incentives: component increases with seniority.
- Benefits value (not cash): health, parental leave, stipends, retirement match, learning budgets.

Benefits highlights (programs available to all permanent employees unless noted):
- Parental leave: 18 weeks primary, 8 weeks secondary (flexible top-up for US-based employees depending on plan).
- Wellness & mental health: unlimited teletherapy sessions via Redwood EAP; dedicated 8 sessions/year covered for dependents.
- Fertility & family support: fertility benefits and adoption reimbursement up to $10,000 (US policy).
- Remote & hybrid support: home office stipend (annual), co-working credits for satellite cities, travel stipend for HQ days.
- Sabbatical: eligible after 5 years (4–8 weeks, role coverage plan required).
- Retirement: 401(k) match up to 4% (US) / country-equivalent programs.
- Education & learning: $3,000 annual learning budget + paid conference days (neighborhood-specific add-ons for research & infra).

Benefits exceptions and special programs

- People-ops pilot programs (e.g., phased return-to-work, enhanced caregiver leave) are reviewed quarterly. Requests come through People Portal and will be handled with manager and People-ops.

Hiring and onboarding — manager and people-ops checklists

Manager checklist (pre-hire):
1. Finalize role profile and neighborhood mapping.
2. Confirm comp band with People-ops.
3. Create hiring plan: scorecard, interview loop, and timeline.
4. Add role to job board & coordinate with recruiting (Greenhouse).

Manager checklist (offer to day 0):
1. Share offer packet and set expectations for start date.
2. Assign onboarding buddy and mentor.
3. Create first 30/60/90 plan and initial deliverables.
4. Notify core teams and schedule kickoff meetings.

People-ops checklist (pre-first day):
1. Create employee record, benefits enrollment window, payroll setup.
2. Provision core accounts: Okta, corporate email, payroll system, People Portal.
3. Trigger access requests for team-specific systems via Redwood Access.

New hire first-week checklist (must-complete):
- Day 0: Welcome meeting, deliver laptop + hardware, verify Okta SSO.
- Day 1–3: Orientation session with People-ops; security & compliance training.
- Week 1: Pair with buddy; complete first 30-day milestones.

Internal tools and access matrix

Below is a condensed access matrix for the most-used systems. Managers must use the Redwood Access request flow for non-default entitlements.

| Tool | Purpose | Default access (new hire) | Owner / Request process |
|------|---------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Okta | Single sign-on and identity | Required | Identity team (auto-provision via People Portal) |
| Redwood Access (internal) | Request & track access to internal services | Read to essential services | IT / Access team (submit ticket or use self-serve) |
| Greenhouse | Hiring & ATS | Recruiters/hiring managers | Recruiting team |
| People Portal | Benefits, payroll, time off | All employees | People-ops (auto-enroll) |
| Slack | Communication | All employees | Admins (auto-join #team channels) |
| GCP Console | Cloud infra | No by default; request per project | Cloud infra (approval required) |
| Datadog | Observability | Read-only default | Eng SRE (escalate for write) |
| PagerDuty | Oncall & ops alerts | No by default; oncall roles | Eng SRE (oncall onboarding) |
| Confluence | Internal docs | Read by default | People-ops / Team owners for write spaces |

Sample CLI snippet — request transient GCP project access

(used by managers to request time-limited read/write access for new hires)

$ r-access request --tool gcp --project redwood-dev-123 --user jane.doe@redwood.com --duration 7d --reason \"onboarding infra access\"

On approval, request ID and expiry are emailed; all approvals are auditable.

Promotion packet — minimal required artifacts

- Candidate statement (manager-written): 1–2 pages of mapped examples to rubric.
- Peer feedback: 3+ peers, written.
- Product/impact metrics: objective metrics where available.
- Development plan and recommended new title/band.

Calibration norms and compensation approvals

- Neighborhood calibration meetings are monthly; cross-neighborhood calibration happens each promotion cycle.
- Compensation changes beyond band midpoints require VP-level sign-off and People-ops approval.

Frequently asked questions (abridged)

Q: How long until a promotion decision?
A: Standard guidance is 4 weeks; exceptions happen for complex cross-org reviews.

Q: Can I request more access before day one?
A: Yes — managers should submit access requests at offer acceptance. Sensitive entitlements (oncall, cloud write, crypto keys) require additional approval.

Q: How do I rotate between neighborhoods?
A: Submit a rotation proposal with objectives; both current and receiving managers must sign off and People-ops must approve.

Operational owners and escalation

- People-ops: overall owner for this playbook, comp policy, and benefits programs.
- Neighborhood leads: own leveling norms, training budgets, and calibration panels.
- IT / Identity team: own access flow and Redwood Access.

Document maintenance and feedback

- Owners: People-ops (primary) and Neighborhood leads (secondary).
- Review cadence: annual review; people-ops will publish interim updates as pilots complete.
- Feedback: use the Confluence comments or ping people-ops@redwood.com.

Appendix: links and templates

- Promotion packet template: /company-handbook/templates/promotion-packet-template-2026
- Rotation agreement template: /company-handbook/templates/rotation-agreement-2025
- Hiring scorecard example: /company-handbook/tools/hiring-scorecard-sample

End of document

- Rotation (3–6 months): short written agreement specifying objectives, mentorship pairing, and success criteria. Rotation count limit: 1 concurrent rotation per individual.

Compensation and benefits — high-level snapshot

Total rewards philosophy: Redwood balances market-competitive base pay, variable components for performance/role, and benefits that reduce hidden costs of high-performance work (child care support, mental health, family leaves).

Compensation components (typical mix by seniority):
- Base salary: 60–85% of total cash (lower in very senior roles).
- Short-term variable (bonus or commission): 0–20% (higher for GTM roles).
- Equity / long-term incentives: component increases with seniority.
- Benefits value (not cash): health, parental leave, stipends, retirement match, learning budgets.

Benefits highlights (programs available to all permanent employees unless noted):
- Parental leave: 18 weeks primary, 8 weeks secondary (flexible top-up for US-based employees depending on plan).
- Wellness & mental health: unlimited teletherapy sessions via Redwood EAP; dedicated 8 sessions/year covered for dependents.
- Fertility & family support: fertility benefits and adoption reimbursement up to $10,000 (US policy).
- Remote & hybrid support: home office stipend (annual), co-working credits for satellite cities, travel stipend for HQ days.
- Sabbatical: eligible after 5 years (4–8 weeks, role coverage plan required).
- Retirement: 401(k) match up to 4% (US) / country-equivalent programs.
- Education & learning: $3,000 annual learning budget + paid conference days (neighborhood-specific add-ons for research & infra).

Benefits exceptions and special programs

- People-ops pilot programs (e.g., phased return-to-work, enhanced caregiver leave) are reviewed quarterly. Requests come through People Portal and will be handled with manager and People-ops.

Hiring and onboarding — manager and people-ops checklists

Manager checklist (pre-hire):
1. Finalize role profile and neighborhood mapping.
2. Confirm comp band with People-ops.
3. Create hiring plan: scorecard, interview loop, and timeline.
4. Add role to job board & coordinate with recruiting (Greenhouse).

Manager checklist (offer to day 0):
1. Share offer packet and set expectations for start date.
2. Assign onboarding buddy and mentor.
3. Create first 30/60/90 plan and initial deliverables.
4. Notify core teams and schedule kickoff meetings.

People-ops checklist (pre-first day):
1. Create employee record, benefits enrollment window, payroll setup.
2. Provision core accounts: Okta, corporate email, payroll system, People Portal.
3. Trigger access requests for team-specific systems via Redwood Access.

New hire first-week checklist (must-complete):
- Day 0: Welcome meeting, deliver laptop + hardware, verify Okta SSO.
- Day 1–3: Orientation session with People-ops; security & compliance training.
- Week 1: Pair with buddy; complete first 30-day milestones.

Internal tools and access matrix

Below is a condensed access matrix for the most-used systems. Managers must use the Redwood Access request flow for non-default entitlements.

| Tool | Purpose | Default access (new hire) | Owner / Request process |
|------|---------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Okta | Single sign-on and identity | Required | Identity team (auto-provision via People Portal) |
| Redwood Access (internal) | Request & track access to internal services | Read to essential services | IT / Access team (submit ticket or use self-serve) |
| Greenhouse | Hiring & ATS | Recruiters/hiring managers | Recruiting team |
| People Portal | Benefits, payroll, time off | All employees | People-ops (auto-enroll) |
| Slack | Communication | All employees | Admins (auto-join #team channels) |
| GCP Console | Cloud infra | No by default; request per project | Cloud infra (approval required) |
| Datadog | Observability | Read-only default | Eng SRE (escalate for write) |
| PagerDuty | Oncall & ops alerts | No by default; oncall roles | Eng SRE (oncall onboarding) |
| Confluence | Internal docs | Read by default | People-ops / Team owners for write spaces |

Sample CLI snippet — request transient GCP project access

(used by managers to request time-limited read/write access for new hires)

$ r-access request --tool gcp --project redwood-dev-123 --user jane.doe@redwood.com --duration 7d --reason \"onboarding infra access\"

On approval, request ID and expiry are emailed; all approvals are auditable.

Promotion packet — minimal required artifacts

- Candidate statement (manager-written): 1–2 pages of mapped examples to rubric.
- Peer feedback: 3+ peers, written.
- Product/impact metrics: objective metrics where available.
- Development plan and recommended new title/band.

Calibration norms and compensation approvals

- Neighborhood calibration meetings are monthly; cross-neighborhood calibration happens each promotion cycle.
- Compensation changes beyond band midpoints require VP-level sign-off and People-ops approval.

Frequently asked questions (abridged)

Q: How long until a promotion decision?
A: Standard guidance is 4 weeks; exceptions happen for complex cross-org reviews.

Q: Can I request more access before day one?
A: Yes — managers should submit access requests at offer acceptance. Sensitive entitlements (oncall, cloud write, crypto keys) require additional approval.

Q: How do I rotate between neighborhoods?
A: Submit a rotation proposal with objectives; both current and receiving managers must sign off and People-ops must approve.

Operational owners and escalation

- People-ops: overall owner for this playbook, comp policy, and benefits programs.
- Neighborhood leads: own leveling norms, training budgets, and calibration panels.
- IT / Identity team: own access flow and Redwood Access.

Document maintenance and feedback

- Owners: People-ops (primary) and Neighborhood leads (secondary).
- Review cadence: annual review; people-ops will publish interim updates as pilots complete.
- Feedback: use the Confluence comments or ping people-ops@redwood.com.

Appendix: links and templates

- Promotion packet template: /company-handbook/templates/promotion-packet-template-2026
- Rotation agreement template: /company-handbook/templates/rotation-agreement-2025
- Hiring scorecard example: /company-handbook/tools/hiring-scorecard-sample

End of document

title: Benefits and Life-Transition Playbook: Hiring, Ladders, and Tools
preview:
Overview

Purpose:
This playbook explains how Redwood supports contributors through major life transitions (new hire, parenthood, relocation, medical leave, sabbatical, retirement) and ties those policies into hiring timelines, role and career ladder expectations, and the internal tools that make transitions smooth. The audience is hiring managers, new joiners, people-ops partners, and people engineering partners who own role design or tool access.

Scope:
Applies to all full-time and fixed-term employees globally. Contractors should consult their engagement contract and People Ops for region-specific exceptions.

Quick reference (one-line summaries):
- Hiring SLA target: 45 days average time-to-offer; expedite pathways for mission-critical roles.
- Onboarding completion goal: 30 days for systems + 90 days to reach expected role impact.
- Benefits changes for life events: notify People Ops within 30 days of event to preserve continuity.
- Tool access: standard kit provisioned in first 24 hours for US/EU hires; 72 hours for APAC hires.

1) Life transitions: benefits, steps, and owners

High-level transitions covered:
- New parent (birth/adoption)
- Relocation (domestic/international)
- Extended medical leave and ADA accommodations
- Sabbatical and phased return-to-work
- Retirement and phased offboarding

For each transition follow this 5-step process:
1. Notify People Ops via benefits@redwood.com (owner: People Ops).
2. Schedule an intake call within 5 business days with the People Ops partner.
3. Complete required paperwork in Workday (or regional HR system) within 10 business days.
4. Review role coverage and temporary reporting / handover plan with your manager (owner: manager).
5. Confirm benefits adjustments and tooling deprovisioning/reprovisioning timeline (owner: People Ops + IT).

New parent (summary):
- Paid parental leave: 16 weeks primary caregiver; 8 weeks secondary caregiver (region adjustments apply).
- Flexible return program: phased hours up to 12 weeks at 80% FTE with prorated benefits maintained.
- Action checklist: submit notice to People Ops, request phased schedule, enroll in childcare stipends (if applicable).

Relocation (summary):
- Relocation allowance and immigration support for company-sponsored moves (teams with recruiting/finance approvals only).
- Tax consultation offered for international moves.
- IT provisioning: confirm network/VPN expectations and private-deploy onboarding if customer data is in scope.

Sabbatical and extended leaves:
- Eligibility: 4+ years tenure for standard sabbatical program (manager + People Ops approval).
- Return-to-work plan must be agreed 30 days before return; People Ops will coordinate role mapping and performance expectations.

2) Roles and career ladders (quick reference)

Principles:
- Ladders are competency-based and outcome-driven.
- Each ladder defines expectations for IC, lead, and manager tiers with sample impact signals and interview calibrations.
- Promotion decisions require a calibration packet (narrative + 3 evidence artifacts + peer manager sign-off).

Summary ladder snapshot (text table):
| Ladder | Typical IC Levels | Typical Manager Levels | Time-in-role guidance | Promotion artifacts |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------|-----------------------|--------------------|
| Engineering | IC2, IC3, IC4, Staff, Principal | Mgr1, Mgr2 | 12–24 months between levels | PR review, roadmap impact, peer feedback |
| Product | Associate PM, PM, Sr PM, Group PM | PM Manager, Director PM | 12–30 months | Metrics-driven PRD, customer signals |
| Ops & SRE | Ops2, Ops3, Lead Ops | Eng Manager | 12–24 months | Runbook ownership, incident postmortems |

Promotion packet checklist (required):
- Narrative (1–2 pages) describing scope and outcomes.
- 3 evidence artifacts (dashboards, PRs, customer feedback, incident reviews).
- Manager endorsement and cross-functional references.
- Compensation review (People Ops) and title /level approval.

3) Hiring & onboarding overview (team manager playbook)

Hiring SLA and metrics:
- Target: 45 days average time-to-offer (advertised to hiring managers).
- Metric breakdown: 10 days to shortlist, 14 days for loop scheduling, 7 days to decision, 14 days to negotiate/offer acceptance.
- Expedited hires: use "expedite request" in Greenhouse with Engineering Director approval; target 21 days.

Stage-by-stage checklist for hiring managers:
1. Requisition approval and role profile (People Ops + Finance signoff).
2. Interview loop design and scorecards (owner: hiring manager + Talent).
3. Candidate funnel hygiene: weekly recruiter syncs.
4. Offer calibration with Compensation & People Ops.
5. Pre-boarding: send welcome packet and hardware intake within 5 business days of accepted offer.

New hire onboarding (30/60/90 template):
- Day 0–1: account creation, security briefing, hardware delivered, orientation session.
- Week 1–4: role-specific ramp, buddy pairing, first deliverable assigned.
- Month 2: mid-rollout check-in (manager + People Ops).
- Month 3: impact review and learning plan.

Pre-boarding checklist (manager responsibilities):
- Confirm start date and time zone.
- Prepare role objectives for first 30 days.
- Identify buddy and schedule intro meetings.
- Submit access requests via Self-Service portal (see Tools matrix below).

4) Internal tools and access matrix (provisioning & deprovisioning)

Standard tools included with role kit (ASCII table):
| Tool | Typical roles with access | Provisioning owner | SLA |
|------|---------------------------|--------------------|-----|
| Okta | All employees | IT Identity | 24 hours |
| Gmail/Workspace | All employees | IT Identity | 24 hours |
| Slack | All employees | IT Identity | 24 hours |
| Jira | Engineering, Product, SRE | Platform Ops | 24–48 hours |
| Confluence | All employees | Platform Ops | 24–48 hours |
| Greenhouse | Talent, Hiring Managers | Talent | On request |
| Workday | People Ops, Payroll | People Ops | 2 business days |
| Internal VPN / VPC access | Eng, SRE, Private Deploy teams | Security | 72 hours (has e-sign NDA) |

Provisioning workflow (high level):
1. Hiring manager submits the role kit request in Self-Service (templates available).
2. IT identity creates accounts and adds to groups.
3. Platform Ops provisions project-level permissions (Jira/Confluence).
4. Security approves VPN or customer-sensitive access.
5. Confirm with new hire and manager; escalate if SLA exceeded.

Deprovisioning & changes:
- Standard deprovision timeline: 48 hours for general accounts; immediate removal for terminated-for-cause.
- Transfer or retention of artifacts: Teams must follow retention policy in security-and-compliance documentation.

5) Manager playbook: what to do in the first 90 days of an employee lifecycle event

Day 0–7:
- Execute pre-boarding checklist.
- Set clear 30-day goals and confirm buddy.
- Run security orientation and data-access training if applicable.

Day 8–30:
- Weekly 1:1s focused on ramp and blockers.
- Assign first small-scope deliverable with acceptance criteria.

Day 31–90:
- 30-day and 90-day performance conversations; document outcomes.
- If a life transition (sabbatical, parental leave, relocation): adjust objectives, confirm coverage, and re-scope milestones.

6) Templates and example artifacts (links and snippets)

Included templates in the internal repo: (request via Confluence attachments)
- 30/60/90 onboarding plan template
- Promotion packet template and checklist
- Role profile template and comp approval form
- Life event notification form (Workday link)

Sample email to People Ops to trigger a life event:
"Hi People Ops,
I am notifying that I will be starting parental leave on 2026-06-01. Please advise next steps and required forms.
Thanks,
[Employee Name]"

7) Ownership, escalation, and related pages

Primary owners and contacts:
- People Ops (owner): people-ops@redwood.com
- IT Identity: identity@redwood.com
- Platform Ops: platform-ops@redwood.com
- Security (VPN/customer access): security-ops@redwood.com
- Talent / Recruiters: talent@redwood.com

Escalation path:
1. Manager -> People Ops partner (72-hour SLA for responses).
2. If unresolved, escalate to Head of People Ops.
3. For tooling SLA breaches, escalate to Platform Ops lead.

Related pages (internal):
- role-framework-and-growth-paths-2025
- onboarding-toolchain-access-and-career-checkpoints-2025
- parental-and-caregiver-leave-policy-2024
- secure-sdlc (for data access rules)

Appendix: Frequently asked questions (abridged)
Q: How soon will my new hire have access to customer data?
A: Only after signed NDAs and role-level approvals. Typical provisioning for sensitive buckets is 7–14 days.

Q: Who signs off on a promotion backfill?
A: Manager + Director calibration + People Ops compensation approval.

Q: What if an international hire needs equipment shipped overseas?
A: Submit a shipping request via Self-Service with at least two weeks lead time; People Ops will coordinate with Finance and Logistics.

Document maintenance: This document is reviewed annually by People Ops and after any major policy changes.

Change log (high level):
- 2024-11-01: Created (Elena Martinez).
- 2025-09-12: Updated parental leave region clarifications.
- 2026-02-15: Updated hiring SLA targets and provisioning SLAs.

title: Role Clarity Playbook for Cross-Functional Contributors
preview:
Summary:

This playbook defines how Redwood documents, assesses, and evolves roles that span multiple domains (for example: product-engineering, design-research, platform-customer-success). Its goal is to reduce ambiguity for individual contributors and managers, accelerate onboarding, and create consistent promotion and mobility paths for cross-functional contributors.

Purpose:
- Provide a single, repeatable process for creating and updating cross-functional role definitions.
- Describe leveling expectations and success criteria that are comparable across tracks.
- Offer a minimally prescriptive template teams can use to document responsibilities, decision rights, and interfaces.

Scope:
- Applies to individual contributor and manager roles that regularly operate across >1 core functional area (engineering, product, design, research, SRE, customer-success).
- Not intended for contractor scopes, short-term rotations (<30 days), or strictly single-track IC roles (those should use the standard role template).

Intended outcomes and success metrics:
- Decrease time-to-clear-role by 25% (time between hire and measurable role clarity documented in HR/Confluence).
- Reduce cross-team escalation incidents due to unclear ownership by 30% in 6 months.
- Standardize promotion evidence: 80% of cross-track promotions use the shared rubric and templates.

Key definitions:
- Role owner: the manager or people-ops partner responsible for maintaining the role page and level expectations.
- Primary track: the functional area that owns the role’s compensation and core career ladder (e.g., Engineering).
- Secondary track(s): other functional areas the role interfaces with regularly and where specialized skills are expected.

Role taxonomy and cross-track mapping:
1) Cross-functional Specialist: Deep in primary track, regular partner with secondary track(s) (e.g., ML infra engineer working with product).
2) Dual-competency IC: Substantial responsibilities in two tracks, title aligns to hybrid scope (e.g., Product Engineer).
3) Interface Lead: Owns handoffs, SLAs, and tooling between functions (e.g., Release Coordination Lead).

Role definition template (copy this to create new role pages):

--- Role title: [short], Level: [Lx], Primary track: [Engineering|Product|Design|...], Secondary track(s): [list]
--- Summary (1–2 sentences): Purpose of the role and primary outcomes.
--- Responsibilities (bulleted): 5–10 items prioritized by impact.
--- Decision rights: Clear, scoped bullets (what this role decides autonomously vs consults).
--- Success metrics: 3–5 measurable outcomes or signals.
--- Interfaces and SLAs: Teams, contacts, expected response/turnaround times.
--- Competencies and examples by level: See rubric below.
--- Typical tools and artifacts: e.g., RFCs, JIRA projects, dashboards.
--- Onboarding checklist: 30/60/90 items tied to role outcomes.
--- Owners: role owner, people-ops partner, peer-reviewers.

Example role snippet (abbreviated):
- Role title: Product Engineer (Dual-competency), Level: L4, Primary track: Engineering, Secondary track: Product
- Responsibilities: deliver feature end-to-end, synthesize product requirements, run A/B analysis, maintain shipping reliability for feature set X.

Leveling rubric (high-level guidance for cross-functional ICs):
- L2 (Foundational contributor): Executes defined tasks with coaching. Demonstrates cross-team communication basics and documents assumptions.
- L3 (Independent contributor): Owns small features/areas end-to-end, negotiates priorities across one other function, demonstrates measurable impact.
- L4 (Senior IC): Shapes cross-functional designs, coaches peers, drives medium-scope initiatives, proactively identifies operational risks.
- L5 (Staff/Principal): Defines cross-team strategy, influences product/tech roadmaps, mentors across tracks, leads complex, ambiguous programs.

Each level should include 3–4 concrete evidence examples. For example, L4 evidence may include: 
- Led a feature that improved MAU retention by X%.
- Negotiated a cross-team dependency removal that reduced cycle time by Y days.

Promotion and role-change process (step-by-step):
1) Initiation: IC or manager opens a Role Change Request (RCR) in JIRA (project: PEOPLE-Roles). Attach current role page, updated role-template copy, and 2–3 success examples. Owner: manager + IC.
2) Evidence collection: Gather artifacts (PRs, metrics, customer feedback, design reviews). Aim for a 6–12 month window of evidence. Owner: candidate and manager.
3) Peer calibration: Present case at monthly Cross-Track Calibration (CTC) meeting. Panel: two senior ICs from each relevant track + a People Ops rep.
4) Decision: People Ops records outcome (approve / ask for more evidence / defer). Timeline: 2 weeks after CTC.
5) Rollout: If approved, update Confluence role page, HR compensation band if needed, and notify payroll/manager.

Shadowing, rotations, and temporary dual-hat guidance:
- Shadowing window: up to 8 weeks of structured shadowing is supported for career exploration.
- Rotation policy: rotations longer than 30 days require a rotation agreement (manager sign-off from both teams and People Ops).
- Temporary dual-hat (<90 days): keep explicit priorities in the role page; escalate capacity/conflict decisions to the primary manager.

Onboarding and the 30/60/90 alignment for role clarity:
- 30-day goals: connect with primary and secondary track partners, document 3 main handoffs, complete role-specific playbooks.
- 60-day goals: run the first cross-team sync as owner, ship a small scoped deliverable, collect early success signals.
- 90-day goals: present onboarding findings to role owner and update role page with lessons learned.

Ownership matrix (example):

| Responsibility                        | Role owner (primary) | Secondary partner | SLA / Frequency       |
|--------------------------------------|----------------------|-------------------|-----------------------|
| Feature scoping                      | Product              | Engineering       | Weekly triage         |
| Implementation & tests               | Engineering          | Product           | Owner rotates per epic|
| Post-merge reliability monitoring    | Engineering          | SRE               | 24–72h response       |
| Release coordination                 | Release Lead         | Product/Eng       | Per-release checklist |
Operational playbooks and templates (links and commands):
- Role-template (Confluence): /confluence/company-handbook/02_roles-and-career/role-template
- Role Change Request JIRA: PEOPLE-Roles -> New RCR ticket
- Quick CLI to export role page to PDF for calibration: export-role --page-id <confluence-id> --format pdf

Examples and patterns (when to create a cross-track role vs keep single-track):
- Create cross-track role when >30% of weekly work is expected to be in secondary track activities, or when decision rights inherently require ownership across teams.
- Keep single-track role when dependencies are transient (<3 sprints) or clearly delegated.

Governance, review cadence, and owners:
- Role pages must be reviewed quarterly by the role owner and People Ops.
- Cross-Track Calibration (CTC) meeting: monthly, chaired by People Ops; attendance from 2 senior reps per track; outcomes recorded and linked to role pages.
- Role retirement: Roles unused for >12 months should be archived after a People Ops review.

Tools, artifacts and templates to use:
- Confluence role-template and playbooks (link above).
- PEOPLE-Roles JIRA project for RCRs and promotions.
- Calibration deck template: /templates/ctc-calibration-deck.pptx
- Evidence checklist: PR links, user metrics, A/B results, customer quotes, design artifacts.

Appendix: common questions
Q: Who sets compensation band for a dual-competency role?
A: The primary track maintains compensation ownership; cross-track adjustments are coordinated by People Ops and Compensation.

Q: How do managers handle conflicting priorities if an IC has two managers?
A: Avoid dual direct managers where possible. Use a single direct manager with a documented partnership agreement and clear priority list on the role page.

Q: What metrics indicate a role is too broad?
A: Sustained >40% missed SLAs, repeated triage escalations across >2 teams, and >30% context-switching overhead reported in weekly time logs.

text:
Summary:

This playbook defines how Redwood documents, assesses, and evolves roles that span multiple domains (for example: product-engineering, design-research, platform-customer-success). Its goal is to reduce ambiguity for individual contributors and managers, accelerate onboarding, and create consistent promotion and mobility paths for cross-functional contributors.

Purpose:
- Provide a single, repeatable process for creating and updating cross-functional role definitions.
- Describe leveling expectations and success criteria that are comparable across tracks.
- Offer a minimally prescriptive template teams can use to document responsibilities, decision rights, and interfaces.

Scope:
- Applies to individual contributor and manager roles that regularly operate across >1 core functional area (engineering, product, design, research, SRE, customer-success).
- Not intended for contractor scopes, short-term rotations (<30 days), or strictly single-track IC roles (those should use the standard role template).

Intended outcomes and success metrics:
- Decrease time-to-clear-role by 25% (time between hire and measurable role clarity documented in HR/Confluence).
- Reduce cross-team escalation incidents due to unclear ownership by 30% in 6 months.
- Standardize promotion evidence: 80% of cross-track promotions use the shared rubric and templates.

Key definitions:
- Role owner: the manager or people-ops partner responsible for maintaining the role page and level expectations.
- Primary track: the functional area that owns the role’s compensation and core career ladder (e.g., Engineering).
- Secondary track(s): other functional areas the role interfaces with regularly and where specialized skills are expected.

Role taxonomy and cross-track mapping:
1) Cross-functional Specialist: Deep in primary track, regular partner with secondary track(s) (e.g., ML infra engineer working with product).
2) Dual-competency IC: Substantial responsibilities in two tracks, title aligns to hybrid scope (e.g., Product Engineer).
3) Interface Lead: Owns handoffs, SLAs, and tooling between functions (e.g., Release Coordination Lead).

Role definition template (copy this to create new role pages):

--- Role title: [short], Level: [Lx], Primary track: [Engineering|Product|Design|...], Secondary track(s): [list]
--- Summary (1–2 sentences): Purpose of the role and primary outcomes.
--- Responsibilities (bulleted): 5–10 items prioritized by impact.
--- Decision rights: Clear, scoped bullets (what this role decides autonomously vs consults).
--- Success metrics: 3–5 measurable outcomes or signals.
--- Interfaces and SLAs: Teams, contacts, expected response/turnaround times.
--- Competencies and examples by level: See rubric below.
--- Typical tools and artifacts: e.g., RFCs, JIRA projects, dashboards.
--- Onboarding checklist: 30/60/90 items tied to role outcomes.
--- Owners: role owner, people-ops partner, peer-reviewers.

Example role snippet (abbreviated):
- Role title: Product Engineer (Dual-competency), Level: L4, Primary track: Engineering, Secondary track: Product
- Responsibilities: deliver feature end-to-end, synthesize product requirements, run A/B analysis, maintain shipping reliability for feature set X.

Leveling rubric (high-level guidance for cross-functional ICs):
- L2 (Foundational contributor): Executes defined tasks with coaching. Demonstrates cross-team communication basics and documents assumptions.
- L3 (Independent contributor): Owns small features/areas end-to-end, negotiates priorities across one other function, demonstrates measurable impact.
- L4 (Senior IC): Shapes cross-functional designs, coaches peers, drives medium-scope initiatives, proactively identifies operational risks.
- L5 (Staff/Principal): Defines cross-team strategy, influences product/tech roadmaps, mentors across tracks, leads complex, ambiguous programs.

Each level should include 3–4 concrete evidence examples. For example, L4 evidence may include: 
- Led a feature that improved MAU retention by X%.
- Negotiated a cross-team dependency removal that reduced cycle time by Y days.

Promotion and role-change process (step-by-step):
1) Initiation: IC or manager opens a Role Change Request (RCR) in JIRA (project: PEOPLE-Roles). Attach current role page, updated role-template copy, and 2–3 success examples. Owner: manager + IC.
2) Evidence collection: Gather artifacts (PRs, metrics, customer feedback, design reviews). Aim for a 6–12 month window of evidence. Owner: candidate and manager.
3) Peer calibration: Present case at monthly Cross-Track Calibration (CTC) meeting. Panel: two senior ICs from each relevant track + a People Ops rep.
4) Decision: People Ops records outcome (approve / ask for more evidence / defer). Timeline: 2 weeks after CTC.
5) Rollout: If approved, update Confluence role page, HR compensation band if needed, and notify payroll/manager.

Shadowing, rotations, and temporary dual-hat guidance:
- Shadowing window: up to 8 weeks of structured shadowing is supported for career exploration.
- Rotation policy: rotations longer than 30 days require a rotation agreement (manager sign-off from both teams and People Ops).
- Temporary dual-hat (<90 days): keep explicit priorities in the role page; escalate capacity/conflict decisions to the primary manager.

Onboarding and the 30/60/90 alignment for role clarity:
- 30-day goals: connect with primary and secondary track partners, document 3 main handoffs, complete role-specific playbooks.
- 60-day goals: run the first cross-team sync as owner, ship a small scoped deliverable, collect early success signals.
- 90-day goals: present onboarding findings to role owner and update role page with lessons learned.

Ownership matrix (example):

| Responsibility                        | Role owner (primary) | Secondary partner | SLA / Frequency       |
|--------------------------------------|----------------------|-------------------|-----------------------|
| Feature scoping                      | Product              | Engineering       | Weekly triage         |
| Implementation & tests               | Engineering          | Product           | Owner rotates per epic|
| Post-merge reliability monitoring    | Engineering          | SRE               | 24–72h response       |
| Release coordination                 | Release Lead         | Product/Eng       | Per-release checklist |
Operational playbooks and templates (links and commands):
- Role-template (Confluence): /confluence/company-handbook/02_roles-and-career/role-template
- Role Change Request JIRA: PEOPLE-Roles -> New RCR ticket
- Quick CLI to export role page to PDF for calibration: export-role --page-id <confluence-id> --format pdf

Examples and patterns (when to create a cross-track role vs keep single-track):

- Keep single-track role when dependencies are transient (<3 sprints) or clearly delegated.

Governance, review cadence, and owners:
- Role pages must be reviewed quarterly by the role owner and People Ops.
- Cross-Track Calibration (CTC) meeting: monthly, chaired by People Ops; attendance from 2 senior reps per track; outcomes recorded and linked to role pages.
- Role retirement: Roles unused for >12 months should be archived after a People Ops review.

- PEOPLE-Roles JIRA project for RCRs and promotions.
- Calibration deck template: /templates/ctc-calibration-deck.pptx
- Evidence checklist: PR links, user metrics, A/B results, customer quotes, design artifacts.

A: The primary track maintains compensation ownership; cross-track adjustments are coordinated by People Ops and Compensation.

Q: How do managers handle conflicting priorities if an IC has two managers?
A: Avoid dual direct managers where possible. Use a single direct manager with a documented partnership agreement and clear priority list on the role page.

A: Sustained >40% missed SLAs, repeated triage escalations across >2 teams, and >30% context-switching overhead reported in weekly time logs.

title: Foundations Toolkit and Role Sprint Handbook
preview:
Overview:

This handbook bundles the practical tooling and milestone calendar for new joiners (ICs and first-time managers) and the Role Sprint process we use to accelerate early-career growth. It is intended to be a single reference for people-ops partners, hiring managers, mentors, and new teammates during their first 120 days and beyond.

Audience:
- New individual contributors (IC1–IC3) and first-time managers.
- Hiring managers and onboarding buddies.
- People-ops and engineering managers who run Role Sprints.

Scope:
- Tool access and minimal config for day‑one productivity.
- A repeatable 6-week Role Sprint format for skill acceleration.
- An aligned set of role-ladder checkpoints and promotion-ready evidence.
- Benefits and perks orientation checklist relevant to first 120 days.

Ownership and SLAs:
- Owner: People Ops - New Hire Experience (people-ops@redwood.ai).
- Operational lead: Asha Mehta (People Ops Partner).
- Tooling owner: Platform Productivity (eng-developer-experience).
- SLA: People-ops acknowledges access requests within 24 hours and resolves standard tool access within 72 hours.

Quick-start checklist (day 0–3):
1) Accounts and access (people-ops action):
   - GitHub (team/org membership), Google Workspace, Slack, Okta enroll, SSO to Console.
   - Confirm access to the following services: repo-read for core infra, onboarding repo write, dev sandbox.
2) Equipment and ergonomics (IT action):
   - Provision laptop, monitor, and dock; ship same-day where regional stock available.
3) Intro sessions (manager action):
   - 30-min role orientation, 60-min product overview, calendar invite for weekly 1:1 cadence.
4) Role Sprint enrollment (manager + mentor):
   - Assign Role Sprint cohort (if applicable) and a primary mentor.

First 120-day running plan (summary):
- Week 0: Getting set up, security training, first demo.
- Weeks 1–2: Shadowing and tiny-owner tasks (bug fix, doc edit).
- Weeks 3–8: Role Sprint (6 weeks) — defined scope, deliverable, and public demo.
- Weeks 9–16: Impact projects and evidence collection for role ladder checkpoints.

Role Sprint (6-week) format (repeatable):
- Objective: 1 focused competency improvement tied to job-level outcomes (e.g., reduce median inference latency by 10% in a small path, or own a documentation chapter and automated test harness).
- Team: new hire (owner), mentor, manager, one reviewer from related team.
- Deliverables:
  1) Sprint brief (1 page): background, goals, success metrics, constraints.
  2) Weekly public standup updates (Slack thread + 1 slide update).
  3) Final demo + PR or doc change + retrospective.
- Evaluation rubric (applied at demo):
  - Outcome alignment (1–5): Did the work map to role outcomes?
  - Quality and craft (1–5): Code/docs meet standards.
  - Collaboration (1–5): Engaged reviewers and cross-team feedback.
  - Velocity/ownership (1–5): Work completed independently and on schedule.
  - Total score guidance: 16–20 = exceeds expectations; 12–15 = meets; <12 = needs follow-up.

Role ladder checkpoints (for IC1–IC3):
- IC1 (Foundational): Demonstrates reliable task completion, basic design awareness, and participates in code review. Evidence: 3+ merged PRs with positive reviews; pair session with mentor.
- IC2 (Developing): Owns a small service or feature, accepts cross-team requests, mentors interns. Evidence: 1+ independent feature, measurable metrics (latency/quality), written post-mortem.
- IC3 (Experienced): Leads medium-scope design, influences roadmap, guides multiple teammates. Evidence: design doc ownership, measurable product impact, consistent peer feedback.

Tools matrix (concise table — use when issuing access requests):
- Tool | Purpose | Typical first-action | Owner
- GitHub | Code hosting and PRs | Fork+build+create PR | Platform Productivity
- Console (Redwood Console) | Observability/rollouts | Link to team dashboard + demo read-only view | Observability Team
- Sandbox (dev env) | Local experimentation | Run sample inference locally | Eng Infra
- Docs repo | Docs and runbooks | Edit onboarding doc and submit PR | Docs Team
- CI/CD (buildkite) | CI pipelines | Trigger baseline test run | Eng Infra

Note: For each tool, the first-action must be completed in week 1 of onboarding. People-ops automates invites via Okta; escalate to people-ops if not received within SLA.

Promotion evidence guidance (how to collect and present):
1) Narrative: 1–2 page summary describing context, actions, and impact.
2) Artifacts: PR links, dashboards, test results, demo recording.
3) Peer feedback: 3+ reviewers' written notes or evaluations.
4) Manager calibration: one-page assessment mapping accomplishments to role ladder outcomes.

Benefits & perks orientation (first 2 weeks):
- How to enroll in health, dental, 401(k), commuter/remote stipends.
- Scheduling a benefits Q&A with People Ops: use the Benefits Portal link in the welcome email.
- Parental leave and sabbatical overview: summary + links to full policy pages.

Hiring & onboarding tie-ins (what managers should do before day 1):
1) Define a clear first-120-day plan and Role Sprint objective in the hiring repo.
2) Pre-assign a mentor and share their calendar availability.
3) Add required reading links to the offer email (product overview, architecture primer, code of conduct).
4) Prepare an initial contribution task that is scoped to 1–3 days of effort.

Manager checklist for running Role Sprints (before sprint start):
1) Approve sprint brief and success metrics.
2) Confirm reviewer and demo audience.
3) Reserve 30–45 minutes for final demo and retrospective.
4) Schedule midpoint check-in (week 3).

Sample sprint brief template (paste into onboarding repo):
- Title: (short)
- Background: (2–3 sentences)
- Goal: (one measurable outcome)
- Success metrics: (quantified where possible)
- Constraints: (services, time, approvals required)
- Deliverables: (artifacts to produce)
- Estimated time: (hours/days)

Accessibility, reasonable adjustments, and remote-first norms:
- We provide captioned recordings of demos and allow flexible working windows for focused work.
- Request adjustments via people-ops-form@redwood.ai; typical turnaround 5 business days.

Offboarding and knowledge transfer (owners must plan at departure):
1) Handover doc with active work list and who-to-contact.
2) Transfer repo ownership and update runbooks.
3) Revoke tool access via Okta ticket (people-ops triggers, SRE validates).

Appendix: useful links and templates (copy into your onboarding repo):
- Onboarding repo template: /repos/onboarding/new-hire-templates
- Sprint brief template: /docs/templates/role-sprint-brief.md
- Promotion evidence template: /docs/templates/promotion-evidence.md
- Benefits portal: https://benefits.redwood.example

Frequently asked questions (FAQ):
Q: How often should Role Sprints run for early-career hires?
A: We recommend one Role Sprint during the first 120 days and another within the first year for high-growth plans.

Q: Who signs off if a Role Sprint score is borderline?
A: Manager and People Ops jointly agree on follow-up actions; the hiring manager documents a 6-week remediation plan.

Contact and escalation:
- New Hire Experience (people-ops@redwood.ai) — first point of contact.
- Tooling escalations: platform-productivity@redwood.ai
- Immediate SLA misses: people-ops-ops@redwood.ai (oncall)

Revision history:
- 2025-09-15: Created; initial publish by Asha Mehta.
- 2025-11-02: Clarified Role Sprint rubric and added tools matrix.
- 2026-02-12: Updated SLAs and added benefits Q&A flow.
