title: Workplace Architecture & Career Ecosystem
preview:
Summary:
This document describes Redwood's workplace architecture and career ecosystem: the company-wide principles that guide how we work, the formal role taxonomy and promotion criteria, a high-level hiring and onboarding flow, benefits highlights relevant to career decisions, and the internal tools & access patterns that enable fast, secure ramp. It is intended for hiring managers, new joiners, people-ops partners, and engineering leaders.

1. Purpose and scope
- Purpose: Provide a single source of truth for how roles are defined, how career progress works across IC and manager tracks, and the operational details that support new-hire success.
- Scope: Applies to all full-time employees and contractors who are on a multi-month hiring/workflow. Does not replace team-level comp bands or role-specific scorecards which are maintained by hiring managers.

2. Core principles
- Role clarity: Every role has a short taxonomy identifier and a concise set of expectations (impact, autonomy, collaboration).
- Equitable mobility: Promotion and role changes are evidence-based and reproducible; bias mitigations and calibrated reviews are required for every promotion.
- Flexible workplace: Support a spectrum of location modalities (remote, hybrid, onsite) with consistent access to tools and career opportunities.
- Developer-first tooling: Access patterns are designed to minimize ramp friction for engineers and product teams.

3. Working models (policy overview)
- Remote-first baseline: Most roles default to remote-first. Onsite presence expectations are defined per role by the hiring manager and documented on the team page.
- Hybrid guidelines:
  - Hybrid (H): expected 2-3 days in the office for collaboration-heavy roles.
  - Core hours: Teams should define a 2-hour daily overlapping window for synchronous work where feasible.
  - Travel stipend: Teams can request ad-hoc in-person collaboration funds via people-ops for quarterly offsites.
- Onsite-critical: Some SRE and Ops roles require regular onsite presence due to hardware and datacenter responsibilities; these roles include a documented accommodation policy.

4. Role taxonomy and levels (summary table)
| Level | Track | Focus | Typical experience | Promotion signals |
|-------|-------|-------|--------------------|-------------------|
| L1 | IC | Early-career contributor, shipping tasks | 0–2 years | Consistent ownership of tickets, learns codebase |
| L2 | IC | Independent contributor, owns features | 2–4 years | Independently delivers features, mentors interns |
| L3 | IC / Eng Mgmt I | Cross-component impact, leads projects | 4–7 years | Leads cross-team projects, measurable product impact |
| L4 | Senior | System-level ownership, shapes roadmap | 7+ years | Leads design reviews, drives operational improvements |
| M1 | Manager | First-line manager, performance coaching | varying | Successful team hiring & development, retention metrics |
| M2 | Manager | Multi-team leadership, strategic planning | varying | Drives org-level outcomes, manages managers |

Notes:
- Each team maintains role-specific scorecards and a short rubric for evidence required at promotion (impact statements, 360 feedback, technical artifacts).
- Promotion reviews require at least two calibrators outside the candidate's direct chain.

5. Career ladder mechanics (process)
1) Self-nomination or manager nomination initiated in the HR promotion tracker.
2) Candidate submits a 1–2 page impact memo and relevant artifacts (PRs, design docs, metrics).
3) Peer and direct-report feedback collected via standardized form.
4) Panel review: Hiring manager, two external calibrators, people-ops partner.
5) Calibration meeting and decision recorded.
6) If approved, compensation and title changes are coordinated with Finance and Talent Compensation.

6. Hiring & onboarding overview (operational flow)
- Hiring cadence: Teams create role requisitions in Lever and attach the public-facing role summary plus the team scorecard.
- Interview design: Every interview loop must include at least one exercise tied to the role's primary responsibilities (system design, debugging, product case study).
- Offer approval: People-ops, hiring manager, and compensation must sign off; for L4+ or director hires, an executive review is required.
- Onboarding flow (first 90 days): See checklist below.

First 90 days — condensed checklist (owner names are placeholders)
1) Pre-boarding (people-ops): laptop order, hardware shipping, calendar invites for orientation.
2) Day 1 (manager): team welcome, role context, 30/60/90 plan review.
3) Week 1 (buddy): repo access, basic walkthrough, join team channels.
4) Month 1 (manager): first feedback checkpoint, shadow design reviews.
5) Month 2 (mentor): active project assignment, cross-team introductions.
6) Month 3 (manager + people-ops): ramp review, set promotion development goals.

Command snippets for common provisioning tasks:
- Okta group request: request via https://okta.internal/redwood/groups with tag "new-joiner" and team code.
- GitHub repo access: GH org request - `gh api --method POST /orgs/redwood/memberships --input ./access_request.json`

1. Purpose and scope

- Scope: Applies to all full-time employees and contractors who are on a multi-month hiring/workflow. Does not replace team-level comp bands or role-specific scorecards which are maintained by hiring managers.

- Role clarity: Every role has a short taxonomy identifier and a concise set of expectations (impact, autonomy, collaboration).

- Flexible workplace: Support a spectrum of location modalities (remote, hybrid, onsite) with consistent access to tools and career opportunities.
- Developer-first tooling: Access patterns are designed to minimize ramp friction for engineers and product teams.

title: Talent activation and care map
preview:
Overview:

Purpose: This document defines Redwood's integrated approach to activating talent, mapping career progression, and aligning benefits and internal tooling so new and existing contributors consistently reach impact faster. It brings together hiring-to-onboard flows, role ladders, perks summary, and the canonical internal tools matrix used by People Ops and managers.

Scope: Applies to all full-time employees, contractors on multi-month engagements, and internal transfers. This is a high-level operational playbook intended for people managers, onboarding specialists, and new joiners.

Owner and contact:
- Owner: People Ops (owner_team)
- Document steward: Jordan Kim (People Ops)
- Questions / feedback: people-ops@redwood.ai

Key principles:
- Predictable first-90 impact: every role has clear milestones for 30/60/90 days tied to systems access and measurable outcomes.
- Role clarity over title inflation: role expectations (what success looks like) are primary inputs for compensation and promotion conversations.
- Tool-first enablement: access to the right tooling on day 0 is the highest-return investment for new joiner productivity.
- Continuous access equity: role changes and transfers must include automated entitlement review and re-provisioning within 3 business days.

Section A — Talent journey map (high level):
1) Recruit & offer
   - Sourcing -> interview -> calibrated debrief -> offer approvals (recruiter + hiring manager + People Ops compensation sign-off).
   - Offer template includes role expectations, initial onboarding cohort, manager 30/60/90 plan, and primary success metric.
2) Day 0 -> first week
   - Day 0: badge, laptop, SSO, email, Slack, HR checklist completion.
   - Week 1: role-specific access (repo, CI, staging), intro with partnered mentor, first small shipping task assigned.
3) Ramp (30/60/90)
   - 30d: orientation complete, first deliverable, feedback check-in.
   - 60d: ownership of a small project area, performance calibration with manager.
   - 90d: independent ownership sign-off, access to broader team decision channels.
4) Growth & mobility
   - Quarterly career conversations, L&D enrollment options, and internal mobility process described below.

Checklist: Day 0 minimum access (owner = People Ops + IT):
- Corporate account + MFA
- Laptop image + dev environment setup guide
- Primary Slack channels and team wiki access
- Code repo read/write where appropriate
- CI job runner queue membership (if developer)
- Console/dashboard read-only for product roles
- Benefits portal and payroll enrollment link

Section B — Role ladder & promotion framework (summary):
- Purpose: Give managers a consistent rubric to evaluate readiness for promotion and to set expectations during 1:1s.
- Levels: IC1 (Entry) | IC2 (Contributor) | IC3 (Senior) | IC4 (Staff) | IC5 (Principal/Leader)
- Evaluation axes (used in promotion packet):
  - Impact: breadth and depth of customer or product outcomes.
  - Craft: technical/professional mastery and the quality of deliverables.
  - Collaboration: cross-team influence, mentorship, and stakeholder management.
  - Autonomy: ability to set direction and deliver with minimal supervision.

Promotion packet required contents:
1. Manager narrative (500–800 words) mapping candidate to rubric axes.
2. Two peer reviewers' written feedback.
3. Example deliverables and metrics (OKRs, PRs, customer feedback).
4. People Ops sign-off on compensation bands and leveling calibration.

Section C — Benefits and perks (concise reference):
- Base benefits: medical, dental, vision (US-standard PPO/HMO options).
- Leave: 16 weeks primary parental paid leave (company policy) + 6 weeks secondary parental leave + flexible unpaid top-up options.
- Flexible work: remote-first with home office stipend up to $1,200 (one-time) and monthly co-working credit program.
- Mental health: 10 therapy sessions covered per rolling year and an EAP line.
- Professional development: $2,000 annual learning allowance; internal conference travel fund for approved contributors.
- Commuter & transit: pre-tax commuter benefits where available by jurisdiction.

Quick reference table — benefits eligibility by employment type:
| Employment type | Health | Parental leave | Learning allowance | Stipend | Eligible for equity |
|----------------|--------|----------------|--------------------|---------|--------------------|
| Full-time      | Yes    | Yes            | Yes                | Yes     | Yes                |
| Contractor (6mo+) | Limited | No           | Pro-rated          | No      | No                 |
| Intern         | Basic  | No             | $500 stipend       | No      | No                 |

Section D — Hiring and onboarding operating playbook (process):
1) Requisition & req approval
   - Hiring manager creates req in ATS with role template, calibrated job score, and hiring panel names.
   - People Ops approves based on headcount and compensation band.
2) Interviewing & candidate experience
   - Standard interview loop: recruiter screen, hiring manager screen, technical/role exercise, cross-functional culture loop, hiring manager debrief.
   - Interview guides live in /company-handbook/interview-guides. Interviewers must complete unconscious bias training annually.
3) Offer -> acceptance -> pre-boarding
   - People Ops issues offer packet with clear expectations, proposed start date, and onboarding cohort assignment.
   - Pre-boarding includes access to reading list, team purpose doc, and first-week agenda.
4) Onboarding cohort cadence (monthly)
   - New joiners grouped by month for cohort orientation sessions: Company mission, security & compliance briefing, health benefits enrollment workshop, and tools deep-dive.

Section E — Internal tools matrix (who provisions what):
- People Ops: HR systems (payroll, benefits portal), learning allowance provisioning, onboarding checklist management.
- IT: device procurement, corporate VPN, MDM, and hardware warranty claims.
- Eng Tools: repo access, CI/CD membership, staging environment credentials (normally granted by team maintainers).
- Security: SSO groups, role-based permission reviews, periodic entitlement audits.

Canonical access SLA (targets):
- Basic corporate account + SSO: 24 hours before start date for scheduled hires, otherwise within 2 business days.
- Role-specific infra/repo access: within 3 business days; escalations route to oncall access-engineer.

Section F — Internal mobility and transfers (process):
1) Expression of interest: contributor opens internal mobility request in the mobility board.
2) Manager-to-manager conversation and release plan: must be documented before offer extension.
3) Transition period: standard 2–4 week handover depending on role scope.
4) Entitlement re-provisioning: People Ops triggers access refresh; IT completes device reconfig if needed.

Section G — Manager checklist and expectations:
- Create a 30/60/90 plan at offer acceptance and share with new joiner.
- Assign a cross-functional mentor for the first 90 days.
- Hold weekly 1:1s and a formal 30/60/90 review with written notes.
- Document promotionable behaviors and provide growth opportunities.

Section H — Measurement & KPIs:
- New joiner time-to-first-merge: target = 21 days (engineering roles).
- 90-day retention for hires: target >= 95%.
- Access SLA compliance: target >= 98% of hires receive base access within SLAs.
- Internal mobility fill rate: target = 30% of open roles filled via internal candidates annually.

Section I — Exceptions, appeals, and escalation:
- Compensation exceptions: require People Ops + Finance sign-off.
- Entitlement delays >5 business days: escalation to Head of IT and People Ops.
- Promotion appeals: contributor may request a peer review board within 30 days of a denied packet.

Appendix A — Sample 30/60/90 template (manager-facing):
- 30 days: Orientation, first bug/issue resolved, meet key partners.
- 60 days: Take ownership of a feature or subcomponent, present a project plan.
- 90 days: Deliver the committed feature, feedback loop documented, proposed areas for next quarter growth.

Appendix B — Helpful links & artifacts:
- Hiring templates: /confluence/company-handbook/04_hiring-and-onboarding/offer-templates-and-approvals
- Interview guides: /confluence/company-handbook/04_hiring-and-onboarding/interview-guides
- Role ladders and rubrics: /confluence/company-handbook/02_roles-and-career/leveling-and-rubrics
- Tools onboarding flow (runbook): /confluence/eng-infra/runbooks/onboarding-dev-environment

Version history:
- 2025-03-18: Created by Jordan Kim (People Ops).
- 2025-08-01: Added benefits table and updated access SLAs (reviewers: Priya N., Luis O.).
- 2026-01-12: Revised mobility timeline and manager checklist; updated KPI targets.

End of document.

title: Benefits activation and eligibility playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook documents how Redwood Inference activates, verifies, and maintains employee benefits eligibility across role changes, onboarding events, leaves, and internal transfers. It is intended for people-ops partners, hiring managers, and enrollment specialists.

Scope: medical, dental, vision, retirement (401k), paid time off (PTO), parental leave, tuition & professional development stipends, commuter/office stipends, and company sabbatical programs.

Quick summary of owner and SLA
- Owner: People Operations Benefits & Enrollment (people-ops-benefits@redwood.ai)
- Escalation: benefits-ops-lead -> Head of People Ops -> General Counsel (for exceptions)
- SLA: new-hire benefit provisioning within 5 business days after first-day completion; eligibility disputes resolved within 10 business days.

Eligibility rules (high-level)
1) New hires\   - Regular full-time employees (FTE) are eligible for core benefits (medical, dental, vision, 401k) on the first of the month following 30 days of service unless local country rules require earlier coverage.\   - Part-time employees: eligibility follows the part-time policy table below.\   - Contractors: contractors are eligible for limited programs (commuter, learning stipend) only when explicitly stated in the contract.

2) Role changes and promotions\   - If a role change does not involve a break in service, benefits continue uninterrupted. People-ops must be notified via the Role Change Form (HR-ROLE-REQ) within 3 business days.\   - Compensation level changes do not change medical eligibility; however, certain perks (executive coaching, expanded stipend) may require manager-requested provisioning.

3) Internal transfers between legal entities or regions\   - Transfers that change the legal employing entity require benefits re-enrollment. The transfer coordinator (hiring manager + people-ops reps) must initiate cross-entity enrollment at least 30 days before the target start date in the new entity.

4) Leaves of absence\   - Short-term leaves (<= 30 days): benefits remain active; payroll deductions continue per normal cadence.\   - Medical/parental leaves > 30 days: people-ops will notify benefits carriers and provide options for paid/unpaid continuation; employees are sent a Benefits Continuation Packet with deadlines and payment instructions.

Table: Eligibility summary by worker type\| Worker type \| Medical \| Dental \| Vision \| 401k match \| PTO accrual 
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| FTE (full-time) | Yes (after 30 days) | Yes | Yes | Yes (up to company cap) | Standard accrual |
| Part-time (>=20 hrs/week) | Yes (subject to local law) | Yes | Optional | Pro-rated | Pro-rated |

4. Employee: completes benefits elections within 30 days (default plan applied if no election).
5. People-ops: confirms enrollment with carriers and verifies access to ID cards within 5 business days of election.
6. Payroll: validates first payroll deduction; report any discrepancies to benefits@redwood.ai.

Submit a Benefits Exception Form to people-ops for retroactive change requests.\- Scenario B: Employee transfers from US entity to UK entity effective July 1.\  Resolution: Initiate cross-entity transfer 30 days prior. Employee completes local enrollment; benefits in the old entity end the day before new entity start. Coordinate tax withholding and payroll timing with finance.

title: First Week Systems & Badge Onboarding Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook describes the recommended, low-friction process for new hires to obtain systems access, physical badge entry, and the core toolchain needed to be productive in their first week. It is intended for people-ops partners, hiring managers, IT/staff engineers, and hiring-team buddies who coordinate access. The emphasis is on automated flows, clear owners, and fallback runbooks for common failures.

Intended outcomes (by the end of week 1)
- Employee has building badge and primary laptop configured.
- Access to core cloud projects, code repositories, and primary communication tools (Slack, email, calendar).
- Access requests are auditable and issued with minimum-privilege roles.
- New hire is able to run the local dev quickstart and join at least one team-sync.

Owners & escalation
- Primary owner: people-ops (badge logistics, orientation scheduling)
- Systems owner: IT Ops / Staff Engineer on-call (laptop imaging, VPN/KMS)
- Access control and IAM: Security team (RBAC policy exceptions)
- Escalation path: People-ops -> IT Ops -> Security -> CTO on emergent risk items

Before day 0 (People-ops checklist)
1. Confirm start date and work location; schedule badge pickup (if office) and shipping of laptop (if remote).
2. Pre-provision SSO account and temporary Slack invite.
3. Generate onboarding access bundle: initial repo access list, cloud project names, and team-specific infra tickets.
4. Create calendar invites for orientation, manager 1:1, and buddy sessions.

Day 0 / Pre-boarding automated tasks
- Trigger account creation job: run `onboard create-account --email <email> --team <team-slug> --start <YYYY-MM-DD>`
- Auto-approve baseline access role: `roles:employee-basic` (access to internal wiki, Slack, and email).
- Push laptop image to courier or on-site pick-up location.

Day 1 checklist (manager + buddy)
1. Welcome meeting and orientation (walkthrough of this playbook)
2. Badge pickup / building entry verification (if on-site): bring government ID; sign H&S brief.
3. Verify SSO login and MFA enrollment. If failure, escalate to IT Ops (see troubleshooting).
4. Confirm Slack and email deliverability.
5. Validate access to source control and package manager: run the quick test in the `quickstarts/new-hire` repo.
6. Run a smoke test with the team’s canonical dev environment (local or cloud).
7. Confirm calendar and team workspace membership.

Core systems access table (summary)
| System | Typical initial role | Owner | Time to grant (SLA) | Notes |
|--------|----------------------|-------|----------------------|-------|
| Single Sign-On (SSO) | employee-basic | people-ops | 0-4h | MFA mandatory; include recovery options |
| Slack | member | people-ops | 0-2h | Auto-joins primary team channel |
| Email & Calendar | standard mailbox | people-ops | 0-2h | Auto-forward rules for manager |
| Source control (git.redwood) | repo-read | hiring manager | <24h | Managers request additional repos via PR |
| Cloud project (dev) | viewer | infra team | <24h | Bump to editor via infra ticket |
| Internal VPN | vpn-user | IT Ops | 0-8h | Required for private regions; certificate-based |
| Badge / Physical access | building-level | Facilities/People-ops | 1-3 business days | Expedited pickup available with manager note |

Notes about the table: the above is a human-readable summary. Individual repos, cloud projects, and sensitive services require explicit tickets or role approvals. Use least privilege by default.

Common first-week operations (how-to)
- Request additional repository access: open `Access Request` in the IT portal, fill repo name and justification, tag manager and security reviewer. Typical flow: manager approves -> auto-grant script runs -> audit log entry created.
- Request production access: production access requires a documented justification and a security review. Use template in the IT portal and attach the rollout plan.

Troubleshooting checklist (frequent failures)
- SSO login fails: ensure new hire registered authenticator; run `auth-tool verify --user <email>` and check error codes. If `ERR_MFA_NOT_ENROLLED`, instruct to register via SSO URL; if `ERR_USER_NOT_FOUND` escalate to people-ops.
- Laptop imaging failed: escalate to IT Ops; temporary loaner policy applies (loaner devices available for <= 14 days).
- Badge not programmed: verify employee ID in facility system, then request reissue with Facilities ticket `FAC-ISSUE-BADGE` and tag People-ops.

Automations and CLI snippets
- Pre-provision role and repo access (people-ops):
  `onboard preprovision --email maria@example.com --teams infra,eng --repos team-repo,shared-lib`
- Manager grant pattern (for recurring contractors):
  `access-request create --user contractor@example.com --scopes "repo:team-repo:write" --expires 2025-04-30`

Security & compliance guidance
- All initial accounts are created with time-limited default roles; any permanent elevation requires a documented business case.
- Audit logs for access grants are retained for 365 days.
- Physical badge provisioning must be linked to a signed offer and background check clearance for roles that require secure-zone access.

First-week success signals (manager report)
- New hire completed quickstart and opened their first PR.
- New hire is in calendar and Slack, attended at least 3 team events.
- No unresolved access blockers older than 48 hours.

Appendix A: Access request template (copy into IT portal)
- Requester: <manager-name>
- Employee: <employee-name>
- Start date: <YYYY-MM-DD>
- Systems required: <list>
- Justification: <short description>
- Expiration: <YYYY-MM-DD or none>

Appendix B: Emergency escalation contacts
- People-ops pager: people-ops@redwood.example.com (watch: 9am-6pm PT)
- IT Ops on-call: it-oncall@redwood.example.com (24/7)
- Security incident inbox: sec-ops@redwood.example.com

Related documentation
- See: /confluence/company-handbook/04_hiring-and-onboarding/manager-first-90-and-career-mobility-playbook-2026 for manager-facing onboarding rhythms.
- See: /confluence/company-handbook/05_internal-tools/onboarding-toolchain-access-and-career-checkpoints-2025 for toolchain-specific deep dives.

Revision history
- 2024-10-20: First draft created by Marisol Chen.
- 2025-06-18: Revised to add CLI snippets and updated badge SLA (Marisol Chen).

title: Cafeteria Beanbag and Ambient Noise Guidelines
preview:
Summary

This page documents a lightweight, experimental set of guidelines for beanbag seating and ambient noise considerations in Redwood Inference shared spaces (cafeteria, lounge, and overflow areas). The goal is pragmatic: improve employee comfort and informal collaboration while keeping operational burden low.

Scope

- Applies to: cafeteria, adjacent lounge, satellite huddle areas on main campus and leased offices.
- Does NOT apply to: focus rooms, phone booths, secure rooms, or private customer meeting rooms.

Guiding principles

1. Respect for teammates: conserve shared spaces for implicit consent — assume people nearby prefer moderate noise levels.
2. Minimal ops: prefer low-maintenance furniture and sensors; avoid complex approvals for small purchases.
3. Data-informed: measure and iterate; use a simple decibel sampling process before large rollouts.

Furniture guidelines (beanbags and soft seating)

- Quantity: limit beanbag count per room to 6 units or 25% of casual seating, whichever is smaller.
- Placement: require at least 1.5m clear circulation path; do not block egress or access to kitchen equipment.
- Materials: choose wipeable, flame-retardant covers rated for commercial use.
- Procurement checklist (sample):
  1. Model spec and SKU
  2. Flame retardant certificate (pdf)
  3. Supplier lead time and return policy
  4. Estimated cost per unit
  5. Maintenance plan (wipe schedule)

Ambient noise guidance

- Target levels: daylight cafeteria 45-55 dB typical; lunchtime peaks allowed up to 65 dB for short periods.
- Quiet windows: designate 11:30-12:30 on Tuesdays as a quiet-lunch pilot in one lounge for two quarters and evaluate.

Quick decibel sampling procedure

1. Tools: smartphone with a basic dB meter app (note: consumer apps vary).
2. Sampling: take 6 samples across a 30-minute window during a target time (e.g., Wednesday 12:00-12:30).
3. Record: document median and max values. Store sample note on the facilities ticket with a short comment.

Example CLI-style sample (for reference in case teams use a measurement device):

# pseudo-command for a USB mic logger (example only)
# record 30s segments and write a simple CSV of RMS dB values
# recorder --device /dev/audio1 --interval 30 --out cafeteria-sample.csv

Operational checklist for pilot rollout (one-month pilot)

1. Facilities ops to order up to 4 beanbags for Pilot Location A.
2. People Ops to publish the pilot notice and expectations (quiet windows, cleanup).
3. Facilities to execute sampling on week 1 and week 4 and upload notes.
4. Survey: send a 3-question pulse survey after week 4 (comfort, noise impact, cleanup).

Safety, maintenance, and waste

- Cleaning: wipeable covers weekly; deep-clean monthly.
- Disposal: follow office recycling guidelines; avoid foam landfill where programs exist.

Decision and escalation

- If complaints > 10% of local headcount in pilot area, pause and review.
- Escalation owner: Facilities Lead (email: facilities@example.com).

Ownership and review cadence

- Document owner: People Ops (Aisha Khan).
- Review cadence: pilot review at month 1, full review at month 3.

Related artifacts and references

- See: Office space guidelines (Confluence) for building rules and safety.
- See: Facilities procurement template (Drive) for ordering checklist.

Attachments and sample forms

- Procurement sample checklist (attach to ticket)
- Decibel sampling notes (CSV)

Notes and known limitations

- This is intentionally lightweight and experimental. It is not a binding policy for client-facing meeting rooms or regulated spaces. If a space is used for interviews or customer meetings, revert to formal meeting-room standards.

Revision history

- 2024-11-07 initial draft by Aisha Khan
- 2025-01-15 pilot parameters updated after facilities review

- Materials: choose wipeable, flame-retardant covers rated for commercial use.
- Procurement checklist (sample):
  1. Model spec and SKU
  2. Flame retardant certificate (pdf)
  3. Supplier lead time and return policy
  4. Estimated cost per unit
  5. Maintenance plan (wipe schedule)

Ambient noise guidance

- Target levels: daylight cafeteria 45-55 dB typical; lunchtime peaks allowed up to 65 dB for short periods.
- Quiet windows: designate 11:30-12:30 on Tuesdays as a quiet-lunch pilot in one lounge for two quarters and evaluate.

Quick decibel sampling procedure

1. Tools: smartphone with a basic dB meter app (note: consumer apps vary).
2. Sampling: take 6 samples across a 30-minute window during a target time (e.g., Wednesday 12:00-12:30).
3. Record: document median and max values. Store sample note on the facilities ticket with a short comment.

Example CLI-style sample (for reference in case teams use a measurement device):

# pseudo-command for a USB mic logger (example only)
# record 30s segments and write a simple CSV of RMS dB values
# recorder --device /dev/audio1 --interval 30 --out cafeteria-sample.csv

Operational checklist for pilot rollout (one-month pilot)

1. Facilities ops to order up to 4 beanbags for Pilot Location A.
2. People Ops to publish the pilot notice and expectations (quiet windows, cleanup).
3. Facilities to execute sampling on week 1 and week 4 and upload notes.
4. Survey: send a 3-question pulse survey after week 4 (comfort, noise impact, cleanup).

Safety, maintenance, and waste

- Cleaning: wipeable covers weekly; deep-clean monthly.
- Disposal: follow office recycling guidelines; avoid foam landfill where programs exist.
